agent_output:
I'll analyze the capacity projections and produce a comprehensive scaling plan. Let me first run the growth numbers to ground this in hard data.

```
Growth projections at 20% MoM compound:
- Month 0:  50,000 orders/day  | DB: 400GB | DB CPU: 70% | Disk: 85%
- Month 3:  86,400 orders/day  | DB: ~690GB | DB CPU: ~121% (saturated) | Disk: 147% (full)
- Month 6:  149,300 orders/day | DB: ~1.2TB
- Month 9:  257,900 orders/day | DB: ~2.1TB
- Month 12: 445,800 orders/day | DB: ~3.6TB
```

The numbers tell a clear story. Here's the full architectural analysis:

{
  "executive_summary": "The system faces a database emergency: at 20% MoM growth, PostgreSQL CPU saturates in ~2 months and disk fills in ~3 weeks. The two identified full-table-scan queries will become unusable within weeks. Immediate action is required on indexing and read-replica offloading (Phase 0), followed by a structured 3-phase scaling plan: optimize (months 1-3), partition and separate reads (months 3-6), and prepare for service extraction (months 6-12). With a 4-engineer team, sequencing is critical — the plan is ordered by blast radius and urgency.",

  "architecture": {
    "components": [
      { "name": "API Tier", "responsibility": "Request handling, routing, auth, rate limiting", "boundaries": "Stateless HTTP layer. Currently 4 instances at 60% CPU peak. Scales horizontally with load balancer. Adequate headroom for ~3 months." },
      { "name": "PostgreSQL Primary", "responsibility": "All reads and writes for orders, customers, deliveries", "boundaries": "Single instance. 400GB data, 70% CPU peak, 85% disk. THIS IS THE CRITICAL BOTTLENECK. Full table scans on order search and delivery aggregation are the primary CPU drivers." },
      { "name": "PostgreSQL Read Replica(s)", "responsibility": "Offload read-heavy queries (search, aggregations, reporting)", "boundaries": "To be introduced in Phase 1. Async replication from primary. Accepts eventual consistency for search/reporting workloads." },
      { "name": "Application Cache Layer", "responsibility": "Cache hot-path reads: customer profiles, recent order lookups, delivery status", "boundaries": "Redis/Memcached. Reduces DB read load by 40-60% for repeat queries. To be introduced in Phase 1." },
      { "name": "Order Search Service", "responsibility": "Full-text and filtered search over orders", "boundaries": "Elasticsearch or dedicated search index. Eliminates the full-table-scan order search query from PostgreSQL entirely. Phase 2." },
      { "name": "Partitioned Order Store", "responsibility": "Time-partitioned order and delivery data", "boundaries": "PostgreSQL native partitioning by month/quarter. Keeps active partition small, enables efficient archival. Phase 2." }
    ],
    "interfaces": [
      { "from": "API Tier", "to": "PostgreSQL Primary", "contract": "SQL via connection pool (PgBouncer). Write path and real-time reads.", "coupling_type": "tight (synchronous, schema-coupled)" },
      { "from": "API Tier", "to": "Read Replica(s)", "contract": "SQL via connection pool. Search, aggregation, reporting queries routed here.", "coupling_type": "loose (can fall back to primary)" },
      { "from": "API Tier", "to": "Application Cache", "contract": "Key-value GET/SET with TTL. Cache-aside pattern.", "coupling_type": "loose (cache miss falls through to DB)" },
      { "from": "API Tier", "to": "Order Search Service", "contract": "REST/gRPC query API. Indexed by customer_id, date range, status.", "coupling_type": "loose (async index population via CDC or events)" },
      { "from": "PostgreSQL Primary", "to": "Read Replica(s)", "contract": "Streaming replication. Sub-second lag target.", "coupling_type": "tight (schema-coupled, version-coupled)" }
    ],
    "data_flow": "Writes flow: API → PgBouncer → PostgreSQL Primary → async replication → Read Replica(s) + CDC → Search Index. Reads flow: API → Cache (hit?) → Read Replica (search/aggregation) or Primary (transactional reads). Order search shifts from DB full-table-scan to dedicated search index by Phase 2.",
    "patterns_used": [
      { "pattern": "Read Replica Offloading", "rationale": "Fastest way to halve DB CPU load. PostgreSQL streaming replication is mature, well-understood, and requires minimal application changes (route read queries to replica connection)." },
      { "pattern": "Cache-Aside (Redis)", "rationale": "Customer lookups and recent order status are high-frequency, low-cardinality reads. Caching these eliminates 40-60% of DB reads with simple invalidation." },
      { "pattern": "CQRS-Lite (Separate Search Index)", "rationale": "Order search by customer is a full table scan — this is a fundamentally different access pattern than transactional writes. A dedicated search index (Elasticsearch or even a materialized view) solves this without distorting the write-optimized schema." },
      { "pattern": "Table Partitioning", "rationale": "At 3.6TB projected in 12 months, queries over the full order table become untenable. Time-based partitioning keeps hot partitions small, enables partition pruning, and simplifies archival." },
      { "pattern": "Connection Pooling (PgBouncer)", "rationale": "As API instances scale from 4 to 8-12, direct connections exhaust PostgreSQL's connection limit. PgBouncer multiplexes connections efficiently." }
    ]
  },

  "domain_model": {
    "bounded_contexts": [
      { "name": "Order Management", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Order", "OrderLineItem"] },
      { "name": "Delivery Tracking", "classification": "core", "aggregates": ["Delivery", "DeliveryEvent"] },
      { "name": "Customer", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": ["Customer", "CustomerProfile"] },
      { "name": "Order Search / Reporting", "classification": "supporting", "aggregates": [] }
    ],
    "context_map": [
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Order Search / Reporting", "relationship": "Published Language — orders emit events consumed by search index. Search has its own denormalized model." },
      { "upstream": "Order Management", "downstream": "Delivery Tracking", "relationship": "Customer-Supplier — delivery tracking depends on order data but owns its own lifecycle and aggregation logic." }
    ]
  },

  "trade_offs": [
    {
      "decision": "Read replicas before sharding",
      "gains": "Fastest time to relief (days, not weeks). Minimal application changes. Well-understood PostgreSQL feature. Buys 3-6 months of headroom.",
      "sacrifices": "Doesn't solve the write scaling problem long-term. Adds replication lag (sub-second but non-zero). Requires query routing logic in application.",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Vertical scaling (bigger DB instance) — buys weeks not months, expensive, ceiling is low", "Horizontal sharding — correct long-term but 2-3 month implementation for a 4-person team, too slow given urgency", "Move to distributed DB (CockroachDB, Citus) — operational complexity too high for team size"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Elasticsearch for order search instead of fixing the query",
      "gains": "Eliminates the full-table-scan problem permanently. Supports rich search features (fuzzy, faceted) that will be needed at scale. Decouples search performance from transactional DB.",
      "sacrifices": "New infrastructure to operate. Index lag means search results are eventually consistent. Team must learn Elasticsearch operations.",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Add composite index on orders(customer_id, created_at) — helps short-term but degrades as table grows past 1TB", "Materialized views — simpler but still lives in PostgreSQL, doesn't reduce DB CPU"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "Time-based partitioning over sharding",
      "gains": "Native PostgreSQL feature, no application changes for writes. Partition pruning dramatically speeds queries with date predicates. Enables simple archival (detach old partitions).",
      "sacrifices": "Doesn't help queries without date predicates. Migration requires careful planning (partition existing 400GB table). Not a solution for write scaling.",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Hash partitioning by customer_id — helps customer-scoped queries but complicates cross-customer reporting", "Sharding via Citus — more powerful but higher operational cost"]
    },
    {
      "decision": "4-engineer team: optimize before re-architect",
      "gains": "Respects team bandwidth. Each phase delivers measurable relief before starting the next. Avoids the trap of a 6-month rewrite that ships nothing.",
      "sacrifices": "The system will carry technical debt longer. Some phases may feel like band-aids. If growth accelerates beyond 20% MoM, the plan compresses dangerously.",
      "alternatives_considered": ["Hire more engineers then re-architect — hiring takes 3-6 months, DB is full in 3 weeks", "Big-bang migration to microservices — high risk, team too small, no headroom to absorb failure"]
    }
  ],

  "flexibility_points": [
    { "area": "Search infrastructure", "evolution_path": "Start with a simple materialized view or even a well-indexed read replica. Graduate to Elasticsearch when query complexity or volume demands it. The API contract (search endpoint) stays stable." },
    { "area": "Caching layer", "evolution_path": "Start with application-level Redis cache-aside. Can evolve to read-through cache, then to CDN caching for public-facing queries. The cache-miss fallback path always works." },
    { "area": "API tier scaling", "evolution_path": "Currently 4 instances. Add instances linearly as needed. At ~15+ instances, consider splitting into order-write and order-read API groups for independent scaling." },
    { "area": "Service extraction", "evolution_path": "Delivery tracking has a natural boundary. Once partitioning stabilizes, delivery can be extracted to its own service + DB. The domain event interface makes this a clean cut." }
  ],

  "rigidity_points": [
    { "area": "PostgreSQL as primary datastore", "reason": "Migrating away from PostgreSQL would be a multi-quarter effort. All scaling work should assume PostgreSQL remains the system of record for at least 18 months. This is fine — PostgreSQL scales well with proper indexing, partitioning, and read replicas." },
    { "area": "Order schema", "reason": "The orders table is the gravity well of the system. Schema changes require careful migration at 400GB+ scale. Plan migrations during low-traffic windows. Avoid wide-sweeping schema changes." },
    { "area": "Monolithic deployment", "reason": "With 4 engineers, maintaining a monolith is the right call. Service extraction is Phase 3 at earliest. Premature decomposition would slow the team." }
  ],

  "risks": [
    { "risk": "Disk fills before any optimization ships. At 85% utilization and 20% data growth MoM, disk is full in approximately 3 weeks.", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "IMMEDIATE: Expand disk capacity or migrate to larger volume this week. This is a P0 operational task, not a scaling project. Additionally, archive or compress data older than 12 months to reclaim 30-40% space." },
    { "risk": "DB CPU saturates during peak within 2 months, causing cascading API failures (connection pool exhaustion, timeouts, customer-facing errors).", "severity": "critical", "mitigation": "Phase 0 (this week): Add indexes for the two identified slow queries. customer_id index on orders table and composite index on delivery history. This alone may reduce CPU by 20-30%. Phase 1 (month 1): Read replica to offload search and aggregation queries." },
    { "risk": "Read replica lag causes stale data in customer-facing search results, leading to support tickets and trust issues.", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Set replication lag monitoring with alerting at >1s. For the order detail endpoint (where freshness matters), always read from primary. Only route search and aggregation to replica." },
    { "risk": "Team bandwidth exhaustion. 4 engineers juggling feature work + scaling + on-call cannot sustain this for 12 months.", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Dedicate 2 engineers to scaling work in Phase 0-1 (months 1-3). Reduce feature velocity by 50% — communicate this to stakeholders now. If growth exceeds 20% MoM, escalate for additional headcount immediately." },
    { "risk": "Connection pool exhaustion as API instances scale from 4 to 8+. Each instance opens its own connections to PostgreSQL.", "severity": "medium", "mitigation": "Deploy PgBouncer in front of PostgreSQL in Phase 1. Transaction-mode pooling supports 10x more API instances per DB connection." },
    { "risk": "Growth rate exceeds 20% MoM, compressing the entire timeline. At 30% MoM the system hits limits 40% sooner.", "severity": "high", "mitigation": "Establish monthly growth monitoring. If 2 consecutive months exceed 25% MoM, trigger early Phase 2 and escalate for vertical DB scaling as a bridge." }
  ],

  "implementation_guidance": {
    "recommended_approach": "Four-phase plan, strictly sequenced by urgency. Phase 0 (Week 1, EMERGENCY): Expand disk, add missing indexes. Phase 1 (Months 1-3): Read replica, PgBouncer, Redis cache, query optimization. Phase 2 (Months 3-6): Table partitioning, dedicated search index, delivery aggregation optimization. Phase 3 (Months 6-12): CQRS for search, evaluate service extraction for delivery, capacity re-assessment.",
    "files_to_create_or_modify": [
      "Phase 0: SQL migration — CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_customer_id ON orders(customer_id); CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_delivery_history_order_date ON delivery_history(order_id, created_at);",
      "Phase 0: Disk expansion — ops/infrastructure change, no code",
      "Phase 1: Database config — add read replica connection string, implement query routing (e.g., read_replica: true annotation on read-only queries)",
      "Phase 1: PgBouncer config — pgbouncer.ini with transaction-mode pooling",
      "Phase 1: Redis cache integration — cache-aside wrapper for customer lookups and order status",
      "Phase 2: Partition migration — ALTER TABLE orders PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at), migrate existing data",
      "Phase 2: Search index — Elasticsearch index definition for orders, CDC pipeline to populate it",
      "Phase 2: Delivery aggregation — pre-computed materialized view or summary table, refreshed on schedule"
    ],
    "patterns_to_follow": "Strangler Fig pattern for search migration: new search queries go to Elasticsearch, old queries continue against DB, gradually migrate all callers. Feature flags for routing between primary and replica. Cache-aside with explicit TTLs and invalidation on write.",
    "pitfalls_to_avoid": [
      "DO NOT attempt horizontal sharding with a 4-person team in the first 6 months — the operational complexity will consume all bandwidth",
      "DO NOT add indexes without CONCURRENTLY — a regular CREATE INDEX on a 400GB table will lock writes for hours",
      "DO NOT cache write-path data — only cache reads that tolerate staleness (search results, customer profiles, delivery history)",
      "DO NOT skip PgBouncer — scaling API instances without connection pooling will hit PostgreSQL's max_connections wall at ~8 instances",
      "DO NOT partition the table in-place on a live system without testing the migration on a replica first",
      "DO NOT defer the disk expansion — this is a data-loss risk measured in weeks, not months"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "missing_constraints",
      "description": "No information on data retention policy. If orders older than 12 months can be archived or moved to cold storage, this dramatically changes the disk and partitioning calculus. At current growth, archival could reduce active DB size by 40%+.",
      "options": ["Assume indefinite retention (worst case — plan for 3.6TB)", "Assume 12-month hot retention with cold archive (reduces active DB to ~1.2TB)", "Assume 24-month retention with purge (middle ground)"],
      "recommendation": "Clarify retention policy immediately. If no business requirement for indefinite retention, implement 12-month hot + cold archive in Phase 1. This is the single highest-leverage decision for the scaling plan."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_constraints",
      "description": "No information on read/write ratio or query distribution. The scaling strategy (read replicas vs write scaling) depends heavily on whether the system is 90/10 reads or 50/50. Full table scans suggest read-heavy, but this should be confirmed with actual metrics.",
      "options": ["Instrument query metrics this week (pg_stat_statements)", "Assume 80/20 read/write (typical for order systems)", "Assume 50/50 and plan for write scaling sooner"],
      "recommendation": "Enable pg_stat_statements immediately and analyze the top 20 queries by total time. This takes 1 hour to set up and fundamentally shapes the scaling strategy."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_constraints",
      "description": "No uptime/SLA requirements specified. The tolerance for maintenance windows affects how we can run migrations (partitioning a 400GB table), deploy PgBouncer, and promote read replicas.",
      "options": ["Zero-downtime requirement (all changes must be online)", "Scheduled maintenance windows acceptable (simplifies migration)", "Degraded-mode acceptable during migration (simplest but riskiest)"],
      "recommendation": "Assume zero-downtime as the target. Use CONCURRENTLY for all index operations, logical replication for partition migration, and blue-green for PgBouncer deployment."
    }
  ],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 58,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Disk is the most urgent risk — the math is unambiguous at 85% utilization with 20% MoM growth",
      "Missing indexes on order search and delivery aggregation are the primary CPU drivers — full table scans are never the right answer at this scale",
      "Read replicas are the correct Phase 1 move — mature PostgreSQL feature, minimal application changes, buys 3-6 months",
      "Team size constrains the plan to incremental optimization, not re-architecture"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact timing of CPU saturation depends on read/write ratio which is unknown",
      "Cache hit rate projections (40-60% reduction) are estimates — actual depends on query distribution",
      "Whether Elasticsearch is needed or a well-indexed replica suffices for search — depends on search complexity requirements",
      "Phase 3 service extraction feasibility depends on how tightly coupled delivery logic is to order management"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "20% MoM growth rate is sustained (not accelerating or decelerating)",
      "The system is read-heavy (80/20 or higher) — typical for order management",
      "No data retention policy exists yet — all data is kept indefinitely",
      "The two identified slow queries (order search, delivery aggregation) account for the majority of DB CPU",
      "The team can dedicate 50% of bandwidth to scaling work for the next 3 months",
      "Infrastructure budget is available for read replicas, Redis, and potential Elasticsearch"
    ]
  }
}

I'll analyze the capacity projections and produce a comprehensive scaling plan. Let me first run the growth numbers to ground this in hard data.

```
Growth projections at 20% MoM compound:
- Month 0:  50,000 orders/day  | DB: 400GB | DB CPU: 70% | Disk: 85%
- Month 3:  86,400 orders/day  | DB: ~690GB | DB CPU: ~121% (saturated) | Disk: 147% (full)
- Month 6:  149,300 orders/day | DB: ~1.2TB
- Month 9:  257,900 orders/day | DB: ~2.1TB
- Month 12: 445,800 orders/day | DB: ~3.6TB
```
